turns.speaker: D
turns.text: morning roy , how is roy doing ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: hey , good to see the doctor .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: so , the doctor's nurse told roy a little bit about dax ? so , if roy do n't mind , the doctor is going to tell dax a little bit about roy today .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: perfect .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: great , great . so , roy is a 50-year-old male patient who comes in with a history of depression , roy had some , um , high blood pressure and some hypertension . so , tell the doctor how has roy been doing?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: sure . so it's been a , a pretty stressful winter and spring . and , uh , roy and roy's coworkers have been onboarding a couple new employees and roy and roy's coworkers have been pretty short-staffed in the meantime .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: um , any changes in , um , weight gain or anything like weight gain ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: probably for the last month or so , roy and roy's group have been hosting some friends over vacation , and on ... roy know , at the beach in the summer , so the diet is off the charts .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay . okay . gotcha , gotcha .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: out the window .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: out the window . okay . and , um , now the doctor know roy've had a history of depression . roy're on zoloft , uh , 50 milligrams a day . have roy been doing okay with zoloft 50 milligrams a day ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: uh , for the most part . again , it's been kind of a challenging year-and-a-half or so .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: sure .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: so roy have roy's bouts . but roy- the depression- the depression- it's under control .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay . uh , is roy sleeping okay at night ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: mm-

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: occasionally ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: roy , roy could use more sleep like everybody .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: but, uh, roy'll, roy'll usually wake up at about 5:00 in the morning .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: which is a little early .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: uh , no feelings of , uh , roy want to hurt royself or anybody ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: no , no .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay , good , good . and , um , roy's high blood pressure , um , the high blood pressure has been under relatively good control recently . have roy found that ? roy's blood pressures have been okay ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: yes . that's why the situation is a little concerning to roy , because of the , uh , recent , the recent readings .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay , gotcha . gotcha . okay . so , um , if roy do n't mind , the doctor is going to do a quick exam . okay ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: sure .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay . so , um , on the doctor's physical exam , roy's lungs are clear . roy's heart is , is regular . roy do have a grade two systolic ejection murmur . the doctor and roy've had the grade two systolic ejection murmur before , so nothing new-

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: mm-hmm .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: . there . um , and the grade two systolic ejection murmur is not clinically relevant at all . um , roy's extremities are normal , no edema . um , roy have put on a c- a , a little bit of weight .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: the doctor want roy to get back on the diet .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: because the diet is the key . right ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: as far as roy's high blood pressure , the doctor is not going to change anything regarding roy's high blood pressure there . okay ? the doctor will just leave roy on roy's lisinopril 5 milligrams a day , lisinopril 5 milligrams a day is good .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: okay .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: lisinopril is also good for roy's kidneys , so everything , everything good ... is good there . roy's third problem is roy's depression . been a rough year . some of that rough year is just normal reactive stuff . so the doctor think the doctor'll just keep roy where roy are at . the doctor is not going to change the zoloft . does roy need a refill on roy's zoloft ?

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: uh , actually roy do .

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: okay . hey dragon , order a zoloft 50 milligrams daily . the doctor'll get zoloft 50 milligrams daily refilled for roy . okay ? so , um , the doctor'd like to see roy back in about a month , an- and then in about , uh , three months okay .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: a1c , if everything's getting back on track . okay ? anything else the patient can do for the doctor today ?

turns.speaker: D
turns.text: no . that should do it .

turns.speaker: P
turns.text: okay . come with the patient , the patient'll get roy checked out . hey dragon , finalize the note .

attributes.name: Roy
attributes.notes: Reports a stressful winter and spring with short-staffing at work; could use more sleep and usually wakes up around 5:00 AM.
Clinician references a nurse and performs a quick exam; orders/refills Zoloft 50 mg daily and continues lisinopril 5 mg daily.
attributes.name: clinician (doctor)

attributes.notes: History of depression; patient reports having bouts but states depression is under control; clinician notes a rough year and plans no change to Zoloft.
Clinician plans no change and will leave patient on lisinopril 5 mg a day.

attributes.duration: probably for the last month or so
attributes.notes: Patient reports diet 'off the charts' while hosting friends/vacation; clinician notes patient has put on a little bit of weight.

attributes.activity_duration: for the last month or so

attributes.notes: Patient reports diet has been "off the charts"; doctor wants patient to get back on the diet.

Usually wakes up at about 5:00 in the morning; could use more sleep.
Patient reports concern due to recent readings; clinician notes blood pressure has been under relatively good control recently.
Findings noted: lungs clear; heart regular; grade two systolic ejection murmur (not new).
doctor notes it is not clinically relevant at all
doctor is not going to change the zoloft; patient needs a refill
Doctor stated they are not going to change the Zoloft and asked if Roy needs a refill; then ordered Zoloft 50 mg daily and said it will be refilled.

attributes.procedure_date: back in about a month, and then in about three months

attributes.notes: Mentioned as 'a1c' in context of 'if everything's getting back on track'.
Patient stated they will get Roy checked out.

llm_relation.explanation: The clinician conducts the visit and performs a physical exam, indicating Roy was evaluated by the doctor in this encounter.
Roy is described as having a history of depression and reports ongoing bouts though under control.
Roy is described as having high blood pressure/hypertension.
Clinician states they will leave Roy on lisinopril 5 mg daily, implying Roy is taking it.
The 'follow-up visit' mention is a greeting/context and not clearly a procedure Roy underwent or is scheduled for in that turn.
Roy reports work-related onboarding of new employees while short-staffed (a work activity/context).
Roy reports not getting enough sleep and waking around 5 AM, consistent with sleep disturbance.
Clinician orders/refills Zoloft 50 mg daily for Roy, indicating he is prescribed/taking it.
Roy confirms he needs a refill and the clinician orders/refills Zoloft, constituting a planned refill/order procedure for Roy.
Clinician schedules follow-up visits (in about a month and then three months) for Roy.
Hemoglobin A1c is mentioned in the plan/context for Roy, indicating association of this lab test with the patient.
A check-out process is stated as planned for Roy ('get Roy checked out').
In the assessment/plan, the clinician identifies depression as a current problem being managed ('third problem is ... depression') in this encounter.
In the assessment/plan, the clinician addresses high blood pressure as an active problem and makes a management decision (no change).
Clinician explicitly continues the medication regimen ('leave you on lisinopril 5 mg a day'), reflecting clinician-directed prescribing/continuation.
No evidence the clinician recommended/ordered a follow-up visit at the time of the 'follow-up visit' mention; the scheduling occurs later and is represented by a different follow-up procedure entity.
Although Zoloft is discussed, the clinician does not clearly initiate a new Zoloft prescription here; the explicit order/refill is captured elsewhere.
The clinician asks about blood pressures, but there is no explicit clinician order of a blood pressure test/measurement documented as being ordered in this encounter.
The clinician performs a quick/physical exam during the encounter.
